vocabulary.meaning: relating to trade, industry, and money
vocabulary.example: The country is facing a severe economic crisis.

vocabulary.meaning: using money, time, etc. carefully without wasting any
vocabulary.example: This small car is very economical on fuel.

vocabulary.meaning: a document showing that money has been paid
vocabulary.example: Make sure you get a receipt for the camera.

vocabulary.meaning: a document showing how much money you owe for goods or services
vocabulary.example: Could we have the bill, please?

vocabulary.meaning: a product made by a particular company
vocabulary.example: What make is your car?

vocabulary.meaning: a type of product made by a particular company, that has its own name
vocabulary.example: It is important to build a strong brand.

vocabulary.meaning: something on sale at a lower price than its true value
vocabulary.example: This jacket was a real bargain at only $20.

vocabulary.meaning: an event or period during which a shop sells goods at lower prices than usual
vocabulary.example: I bought these boots in the January sales.

vocabulary.meaning: a reduction in the usual price of something
vocabulary.example: They offer a 10% discount to students.

vocabulary.meaning: a special reduced price or opportunity for a limited time
vocabulary.example: The shop has a special offer on digital cameras.

vocabulary.meaning: the amount of money for which something is sold
vocabulary.example: The price of petrol has gone up again.

vocabulary.meaning: the amount of money needed to buy, do, or make something
vocabulary.example: We have to keep the cost of the project down.

vocabulary.meaning: coins rather than paper money, or the money returned when paying more than the price
vocabulary.example: Here is your change and your receipt.

vocabulary.meaning: money in the form of notes and coins
vocabulary.example: Will you be paying in cash or by credit card?

vocabulary.meaning: a large amount of money or valuable possessions
vocabulary.example: His wealth is estimated at over $100 million.

vocabulary.meaning: a large amount of money
vocabulary.example: She made a fortune from her business.

vocabulary.meaning: a drawer for money in a shop
vocabulary.example: Please pay at the till.

vocabulary.meaning: the place in a shop where you pay for goods
vocabulary.example: There was a long queue at the checkout.

vocabulary.meaning: things that are made to be sold or used
vocabulary.example: They sell a wide range of organic products.

vocabulary.meaning: possessions and items that can be moved and sold
vocabulary.example: The company produces leather goods.

vocabulary.meaning: to give back money, especially because goods are not satisfactory
vocabulary.example: We will refund your money if you are not satisfied.

vocabulary.meaning: to give something and receive something of the same kind in return
vocabulary.example: Can I exchange this shirt for a larger one?

vocabulary.meaning: not real, made to look like something valuable or genuine in order to deceive
vocabulary.example: He was arrested for using a fake passport.

vocabulary.meaning: made of plastic
vocabulary.example: We should avoid using single-use plastic bags.

vocabulary.meaning: an official document listing goods or services provided and their prices
vocabulary.example: We sent them an invoice for the work completed.

vocabulary.meaning: the department that sells a company's products, or the amount of goods sold
vocabulary.example: Our sales have increased by 15% this month.

vocabulary.meaning: impossible to defeat or improve upon; extremely good value
vocabulary.example: Our prices are unbeatable.

vocabulary.meaning: soap in the form of powder used for washing clothes
vocabulary.example: She put a scoop of washing powder into the washing machine.

vocabulary.meaning: a large bag made of strong material
vocabulary.example: They bought a sack of potatoes.

vocabulary.meaning: a legal document stating a person's wishes regarding the disposal of their property after death
vocabulary.example: He left all his money to charity in his will.

vocabulary.meaning: a space or room just below the roof of a house
vocabulary.example: We stored our old suitcases in the attic.

vocabulary.meaning: a ring given to someone to show that they are engaged to be married
vocabulary.example: She showed her friends her beautiful engagement ring.

vocabulary.meaning: very thin slices of potato that have been fried till crunchy and eaten cold
vocabulary.example: He ate a packet of crisps with his lunch.

vocabulary.meaning: a time of intense difficulty, trouble, or danger
vocabulary.example: The country is facing an economic crisis.

vocabulary.meaning: move in the direction of
vocabulary.example: We decided to head for the beach.

vocabulary.meaning: activity done for enjoyment when one is not working
vocabulary.example: The park is used for leisure and recreation.

vocabulary.meaning: money paid regularly at a particular rate for the use of money lent
vocabulary.example: The bank charges high interest on loans.

vocabulary.meaning: a television or radio programme about current political or social issues
vocabulary.example: He likes watching current affairs programmes on TV.

phrasalVerbs.example: EX: I'm banking on getting a promotion next month.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: find something or meet someone by chance
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: I came across an old photo of my parents in the drawer.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: get something, especially something that is hard to get
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: Good jobs are hard to come by these days.
EX: She came into a lot of money when her grandfather died.
EX: We will have to do without a car for a while.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: manage to survive or live financially
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: It is difficult to get by on such a low salary.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: use all of something, or finish something
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: We got through a lot of money on our holiday.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: give something for free, or reveal a secret
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: They are giving away free samples of the new perfume.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: use money to buy the things you need in life
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: They have to live on his pension.
EX: We spent the afternoon looking round the old town.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: write all the necessary information on a cheque, etc.
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: Who should I make the cheque out to?

phrasalVerbs.meaning: provide something good to balance or reduce the bad effect of something else
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: The nice weather made up for the bad hotel.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: save money for a later time
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: We try to put by a little money every month for our holiday.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: save money for a specific purpose
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: She is saving up for a new laptop.

collocations.meaning: a quantity of something
in large or small quantities
add up to a total
ask someone to pay a certain amount
pay an amount of money for a service or privilege
accept responsibility for something
responsible for or in control of something
wanted by a lot of people
done or available whenever someone asks
a strong request or need for something
owing money to someone
start to owe money
pay off a debt completely

collocations.collocation: owe sb a debt of gratitude
collocations.meaning: feel very grateful to someone for their help
have a sufficient quantity of something
have sufficient things to perform an action
used to say that you will not tolerate any more of something
paid for by oneself rather than someone else
spend money on something
costs incurred in the running of a business
an arrangement where an employer pays for business-related travel, meals, etc.
earn a large amount of money
earn a lot of money
be extremely expensive
become rich by working hard or being successful

collocations.collocation: an increase in sth of (a certain amount)
collocations.meaning: a rise in the amount or level of something
an increase in salary
a rise in the cost of something
not less than; at the minimum
used to emphasize that a quantity is the absolute minimum
importantly, despite being mentioned after everyone else
used to suggest that the reality is more extreme than what you are saying
obtain, keep, or possess money
use money to buy goods or services
not having enough money
perform an action solely to earn money
observe someone performing an action
pay attention to something
only a short time before something happens
inform someone in advance about something
earn more money than you spent from a transaction
keep money instead of spending it
do something faster to have more time available
keep something to use at a future time
buy food and other items for the household
visit shops to buy things
a large building containing many shops
looking at goods in shop windows without buying them

wordPatterns.meaning: have enough money to perform an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: I can't afford to buy a new car.

wordPatterns.meaning: speak angrily to someone because you disagree
wordPatterns.example: EX: Don't argue with your parents.

wordPatterns.meaning: disagree angrily about a topic or action
wordPatterns.example: EX: They are always arguing about money.

wordPatterns.meaning: give reasons to support an opinion
wordPatterns.example: EX: The economist argued that taxes should be lowered.

wordPatterns.meaning: ask someone for something in an anxious or humble way
wordPatterns.example: EX: They begged us for help.

wordPatterns.meaning: ask someone anxiously to perform an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: She begged him not to leave.

wordPatterns.meaning: be the property of someone or something
wordPatterns.example: EX: This book belongs to the library.

wordPatterns.meaning: take and use something belonging to someone else with the intention of returning it
wordPatterns.example: EX: Can I borrow a pen from you?

wordPatterns.meaning: ask someone for money in exchange for something or an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: The hotel charged us $10 for breakfast.

wordPatterns.meaning: ask for something forcefully from someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: They demanded an apology from him.

wordPatterns.meaning: request forcefully that something should happen
wordPatterns.example: EX: The workers demanded that their wages be increased.

wordPatterns.meaning: fail to remember to perform a planned action
wordPatterns.example: EX: Don't forget to lock the door when you leave.

wordPatterns.meaning: fail to remember a past event or action
wordPatterns.example: EX: I will never forget visiting Paris for the first time.

wordPatterns.meaning: stop thinking about a topic or action
wordPatterns.example: EX: Let's forget about work and enjoy our dinner.

wordPatterns.meaning: not remember if a statement is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: I forget whether they have got children or not.

wordPatterns.meaning: allow someone to use something on the understanding that they will return it
wordPatterns.example: EX: She lent her car to her brother.

wordPatterns.meaning: allow someone to use your property temporarily
wordPatterns.example: EX: Can you lend me your pen for a second?

wordPatterns.meaning: give someone money for their work or services
wordPatterns.example: EX: He paid the builder for repairing the roof.

wordPatterns.meaning: give an amount of money to someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: You have to pay the rent to the landlord tomorrow.

wordPatterns.meaning: gain an advantage or money from a situation or action
wordPatterns.example: EX: Many companies profited from the new law.

wordPatterns.meaning: rescue someone from danger or a bad situation
wordPatterns.example: EX: The doctor saved him from dying.

wordPatterns.meaning: keep something for a particular purpose or person
wordPatterns.example: EX: She saved some cake for her children.

wordPatterns.meaning: use money or time on something or an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: He spent $50 on a new shirt.

wordFormation.meaning: accept: consent to receive or undertake / acceptance: the action of consenting to receive / accepting: open or welcoming / (un)acceptable: (not) able to be agreed to or suitable
wordFormation.example:
EX: Please accept our apologies.
EX: The university sent her a letter of acceptance.
EX: She is very accepting of other cultures.
EX: His behaviour was completely unacceptable.

wordFormation.meaning: assist: help someone / assistance: help or support / assistant: a person who helps in a particular job
wordFormation.example:
EX: She assisted the doctor with the patient.
EX: Can I be of any assistance?
EX: He works as a shop assistant.

wordFormation.meaning: day: a period of 24 hours / everyday: happening or used every day; common / daily: occurring every day
wordFormation.example:
EX: It was a beautiful sunny day.
EX: Stress is a part of everyday life.
EX: The newspaper is published daily.

wordFormation.meaning: economy: the state of a country's trade and industry / economics: the study of trade and industry / economist: a person who studies economics / economic: relating to trade and industry / (un)economical: (not) avoiding waste or spending more than necessary
wordFormation.example:
EX: The economy is growing steadily.
EX: She decided to study economics at university.
EX: He is a well-known economist.
EX: The country is facing economic problems.
EX: It is uneconomical to run the heating all day.

wordFormation.meaning: end: finish or make something finish / ending: the last part of a story, film, etc. / unending: continuing forever or seeming to / endless: having or seeming to have no end
wordFormation.example:
EX: The meeting ended at 5 p.m.
EX: The film has a happy ending.
EX: They faced unending difficulties.
EX: We had an endless discussion about it.

wordFormation.meaning: finances: the money that a person, company, or government has / financial: relating to money
wordFormation.example:
EX: We need to manage our finances carefully.
EX: The company is facing financial difficulties.

wordFormation.meaning: expense: the cost of something / expenses: money spent on travel, meals, etc. while working / expenditure: the action of spending funds / (in)expensive: costing (not) a lot of money
wordFormation.example:
EX: We went to a lot of expense for the wedding.
EX: You can claim your travel expenses.
EX: The government's expenditure on education has increased.
EX: This watch is very expensive.

wordFormation.meaning: invest: put money into financial schemes with the expectation of achieving a profit / investor: a person who invests money / investment: the action of investing money
wordFormation.example:
EX: He decided to invest in the stock market.
EX: They are trying to attract foreign investors.
EX: The new factory is a major investment.

wordFormation.meaning: luxury: state of great comfort and extravagant living / luxuries: expensive and unnecessary things / luxurious: extremely comfortable, elegant, or expensive
wordFormation.example:
EX: They live in luxury.
EX: We can't afford such luxuries.
EX: They stayed at a luxurious hotel.

wordFormation.meaning: pay: give someone money for work, goods, or services / payment: the action of paying / payable: required to be paid
wordFormation.example:
EX: How much did you pay for this shirt?
EX: The bank received the payment yesterday.
EX: The rent is payable on the first of the month.

wordFormation.meaning: poor: having little money / poverty: the state of being extremely poor / poorly: in a poor or tồi tàn way
wordFormation.example:
EX: He grew up in a poor family.
EX: Millions of people live in poverty.
EX: The children were poorly dressed.

wordFormation.meaning: real: existing in fact / (un)real: (not) real / reality: the state of things as they actually exist / realisation: the act of realizing something / realise: become fully aware of something / (un)realistic: (not) sensible or practical / really: in fact or truth
wordFormation.example:
EX: This is a real gold watch.
EX: It felt unreal when we won.
EX: We must face reality.
EX: The plan was brought to realisation.
EX: I suddenly realised I had lost my keys.
EX: It is unrealistic to expect them to finish today.
EX: I really don't know what to do.

wordFormation.meaning: value: the worth of something / valuation: an estimation of the worth of something / (in)valuable: (not) worth a great deal of money / valueless: having no value
wordFormation.example:
EX: The value of the house has increased.
EX: They obtained a valuation of the property.
EX: She gave me a valuable gold ring.
EX: His advice was invaluable to us.
EX: The painting was found to be valueless.

wordFormation.meaning: wealth: a large amount of money or possessions / wealthy: rich
wordFormation.example:
EX: His wealth has grown over the years.
EX: They are a wealthy family.
